# Questions in Livestream 134

Live Stream #134: Home Sweet Home

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:11</a> | If Rameses II's firstborn died around 1254 BC, why is that not the date of the Exodus? | He said the answer depends on the starting chronology for Rameses II; newer chronologies can push the approximate Exodus date earlier, around 1263-1265 BC. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">9:01</a> | Why do most ancient Near Eastern texts and literature never achieve final redaction or canonization, but instead have multiple versions? | He pointed to scribal transmission: hand copying, updating, and copying errors create textual variants before later ideas of fixed manuscript forms develop. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=863" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:23</a> | Are there books on Genesis he recommends besides Walton? | He could not recommend a specific overview off the top of his head and said he usually goes to Walton or to commentaries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1001" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:41</a> | Does Walton overstate his case about ancient Near Eastern material and functional origins? | He said Genesis and origins are so complex that any scholar may eventually overstate a case, though he did not generally accuse Walton of doing so. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1412" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:32</a> | Why are there more stories of Christian biblical scholars losing faith, such as Bart Ehrman, than secular scholars coming to faith? | He argued the shift usually happens while people are students, before they become scholars, and that both conversion and deconversion can occur during that formation period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1717" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:37</a> | How should Jude's use of non-canonical sources such as the Assumption of Moses and 1 Enoch be understood? | He said Jude uses familiar cultural material to support a narrow point, not to declare those sources inspired or fully historically authoritative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=1996" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:16</a> | How should Genesis 6:1-4 be interpreted, and does the relative clause in 6:4 refer to "those days" or "afterward"? | He treated the passage cautiously and emphasized following the wording and structure of the text rather than importing later speculative readings. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=5636" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:56</a> | What cities are safe to travel in Egypt today? | He said many cities are relatively safe, but warned strongly against dangerous regions such as Sinai. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6574" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:34</a> | How radically different were First and Second Temple period theologies, and how much influence did the Babylonian exile have? | He said the exile had significant influence on Second Temple theology and helped produce many of the differences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=6903" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:55:03</a> | If Jude quotes Enoch, is he asserting that Enoch truly prophesied the quoted prophecy? | Not necessarily. He said Jude is making a narrow argument with the quoted material, not endorsing all of Enoch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9348" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:48</a> | Was he saying Early Bronze Age? | No. He clarified that he meant Late Bronze to early Iron I, not Early Bronze Age. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=9420" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:00</a> | Which of Hess's works discusses early second-millennium Yahweh names? | He was not sure it had been published and remembered hearing the paper at an ASOR meeting around 2017 or 2018. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=10601" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:56:41</a> | Did he talk with David Wood? | Only briefly. He passed him at the convention center, and IP wanted to facilitate more conversation later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=12734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:14</a> | Does the subtle theme of sacred and profane space across Scripture seem divinely inspired? | He agreed, saying that theme is woven through the arc of Scripture from creation onward. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13519" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:45:19</a> | When will he shave off the goatee? | Never, he joked; his producer would not let him shave it off. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=13940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:52:20</a> | What dissertation or study topics in Egyptology would give valuable insights and help the church? | He said there are many possibilities, including death in the garden and related Egyptology-Bible themes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15066" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:11:06</a> | How would ancient Egyptians react to modern pop-culture depictions of mummies resurrecting as monsters? | He compared it to people digging up graves and using remains disrespectfully, so Egyptians would likely find it offensive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15275" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:14:35</a> | Why did the whole family have to remain inside during the Passover rather than just mark the door and leave the firstborn at home? | He answered in terms of communal guilt: the household needed to be under the sign together. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15490" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:18:10</a> | Has he seen any more ghosts at work? | No, not since changing jobs; the earlier workplace was the one with the strange experiences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/HgdAiK9HH-A?t=15949" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:49</a> | How should ancient Near Eastern context be used in biblical theology, and where does it go too far? | He said context is valuable, but it goes too far when cosmetic parallels become parallelomania or when scholars overbuild dependency claims. |
